sections.text: The ballad begins with epithets of praise for King Kitekere (lines 1-8).
During the night, a spirit medium is roused unwillingly from a sleep by his youngest wife (lines 9-21).
They argue (lines 22-37).
The elder wives ask the medium about the contents of his dream but receive a curt rebuff (lines 38-49).
The wives help prepare the spirit medium for his journey to the king (lines 50-65)
The spirit medium travels, eventually meeting a ferryman to whom he indicates his mission and provides payment (lines 66-82).
The spirit medium travels on through several landmarks (lines 83-98).
At the outskirts of the palace compound, the guards he meets question the feasibility of his mission, and he answers them, demonstrating his knowledge of court life (lines 99-140).

On being asked, the guards tell the spirit medium that the king is in a grove playing the game of mankala. The spirit medium proceeds towards the palace and sees warriors practicing military skills (lines 141-160).
He meets the guardian of the royal court and provides gifts. The spirit medium proceeds to an audience with the king (line 161-183).
He greets the king and presents his prophecy that warns about a particular warrior. The king receives the information favorably (lines 184-213).
An alarm is heard, and guesses as to its origin in the hunt are offered and dismissed with arguments based on conventions of poetic praise (lines 213-243).
Finally, a cowherd arrives and with great emotion reports a cattle raid to the king (lines 244-61).
The king responds, recognizing the truth of the prophecy by repeating part of it. Then the cowherd reports the results of an attack on several villages (lines 262-282).
The king responds first with emotion then with heroic gestures that foretell action (lines 283-294).
The king prepares for battle first by consulting diviners. Then he gives orders to household servants to prepare provisions (lines 295-325).
The warriors present at court chant their self-praise to prepare for battle (lines 326-50).
The king sets out. He pauses to comment on some monkeys as symbols of the ruling dynasties of other kingdoms Another warrior chants his praise (lines 351-377).
The king summons particular warriors and names the battalions each should serve with (lines 378-395).
The king attacks Kihanja kingdom. Scenes of battle are described. The army moves on and the king orders particular villages attacked (lines 396-419).
After victorious battle, several warriors chant their self-praise (lines 421-460).
The army travels further into Kihanja, attacks other settlements, and crosses into Ihangiro Kingdom (lines. Another scene of men in battle is described (461-497).
The king of Ihangiro is heard to utter words of fear and awe for Kitekere and to order that tribute be paid him. Royal women of Ihangiro comment on Kitekere's manliness (lines 498-518).
Kitekere decides to stay temporarily in Ihangiro, receives tribute from the king there, and threatens further attack. His men counsel peace (lines 519-536).
The army stays to harvest its millet and then returns home. The self-praise of victorious warriors is heard again (lines 537-558).
In a coda, the singer comments on the punishment of the miscreant and envisions the descendants of Kitekere walking back to Kyamutwara Kingdom (lines 559-566
